title: Media Hutch (Rebecca Media Suite)
summary: A plywood media hutch topped with mitered crown moulding — a compact box build where the crown trim is the one genuinely fussy detail.
description:
The carcass is a simple plywood box — top, sides, a shelf, dividers, and a back — built the same way as any bookshelf-style cabinet. What sets this apart visually is the crown moulding wrapping the top, and crown is the one moulding profile that punishes a rushed miter more than any other: because it sits at an angle against the wall and ceiling (or in this case, against the top of the cabinet), a crown miter has to account for that spring angle, and a miter cut as if it were flat trim will leave a visible gap at the joint.

Build and attach the face frame before the back goes on, and dry-fit the crown corners on the bench before nailing anything — cutting a test miter in scrap first, at whatever angle your crown actually rests, is cheap insurance against wasting the real moulding on a bad cut.

tools.note: Test-cut crown moulding corners in scrap first — the spring angle makes a crown miter behave differently than flat trim.
For ripping the plywood parts to width.

cutList.note: Cut on miter — verify your own measurements before cutting.
Cut on miter — verify your own measurements before cutting.

steps.title: Cut the case and frame parts
steps.body: Break the 3/4" plywood into 11 3/4"-wide strips, then crosscut two sides 46 3/4", the top 57 1/2", the shelf 52 1/2", and the divider 10 3/4". Cut with the good face up and support the offcut so the sheet cannot twist as it comes free. From the pine cut two face frame stiles 43 1/2", the top rail 51", and the bottom rail 57 1/2" from the 1x2, and the 52 1/2" back support from the 1x3. Leave the crown moulding uncut until the case is standing: its three pieces total 81" against the 83" of stock supplied, so there is almost nothing to spare for a mis-cut miter.

steps.title: Attach the top to the sides
steps.body: Stand the two 46 3/4" sides on edge and join the 57 1/2" top to them with glue and pocket-hole screws or countersunk screws driven down through the top. The plan's own numbers do not settle whether the top caps the sides or sits between them — the 52 1/2" shelf closes on neither reading — so dry-fit it, decide now, and keep the shelf and divider consistent with whatever you chose. Check both diagonals across the assembly and rack it until they match before the glue sets.

steps.title: Add the shelf and divider
steps.body: Set the 52 1/2" shelf across the case at the height your components need, and stand the 10 3/4" divider on it to split the lower bay. If the shelf does not land tight against both sides, trim it to the inside measurement you actually have — your case is the governing number, not the cut list. Fasten both with pocket holes drilled on their undersides and hidden faces, plus glue, and hold a square where the divider meets the shelf; the divider is short enough that a small lean is easy to miss and will show against the face frame later.

steps.title: Add the back support
steps.body: Run the 52 1/2" back support across the back of the case, flush with the back edges of the sides, and fasten it with glue and pocket-hole screws into each side. It does two jobs: it stiffens the case against racking, and it gives the thin plywood back something solid to nail into. Put it where a brad nailer can reach it once the back goes on, and check the case for square again after it is fastened — a support fitted into a racked case will hold the rack permanently.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame flat on the bench first — the two 43 1/2" stiles, the 51" top rail, and the 57 1/2" bottom rail — joined with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" screws. These four lengths do not add up to one rectangle: 51" between two 1 1/2" stiles is 54", while the bottom rail is 57 1/2". So lay the parts out against the finished case front, mark the rails there, and cut them to what you actually measure. Once the frame is square, glue it to the front of the case and pin it with 1 1/4" brads, keeping the outside edges flush with the case sides.

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: Cut the 1/4" plywood back to the outside dimensions of the assembled case — measure the case, because the cut list's only 'Back panel' row is a 3/4" x 3 1/2" pine board and gives you no panel size. Nailing the back on is what locks the case square, so check the diagonals one last time and rack the case gently by hand until they match before driving more than the first few nails. Run glue along the sides, top, shelf and back support, then pin the panel with 1 1/4" brads every few inches.

steps.title: Cut and attach the crown moulding
steps.body: Cut a test miter in scrap moulding first, at whatever spring angle your crown rests at — this is cheap insurance against a wasted cut on the real material. Once the test corner closes cleanly, miter the front and side crown pieces and attach around the top of the cabinet.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every surface through the assorted grits, leaving the face frame and the crown until last so you do not round over the mitered corners. Ease the front edges of the top and shelf slightly — a crisp plywood arris chips the first time something is dragged across it. Vacuum and wipe the dust off, then prime and paint or stain and seal. Finish the inside of the bays too; they get handled every time a component is swapped.

images.alt: Media hutch with mitered crown moulding trim
